title: Communication Patterns for Engineers
summary: The patterns that make technical communication work — from ADRs and RFCs to stakeholder updates, incident comms, and pull request descriptions that actually get merged.

chapters.title: Why Communication Is Engineering Work
chapters.summary: Code is half the job. The engineer who can't explain their work loses influence, creates technical debt in documentation, and ships work that doesn't get used. Communication is a learnable engineering skill.

chapters.title: Knowing Your Audience
chapters.summary: Who's reading, what context they bring, and what they need to walk away with — the three questions that shape every technical communication before you write a word.

chapters.title: Writing Patterns — ADRs, RFCs, and Design Docs
chapters.summary: The recurring document types in engineering teams — their purpose, format, lifecycle, and the workflow that keeps them from going stale the week after they're written.

chapters.title: The Pyramid Principle for Technical Writing
chapters.summary: Answer first, support second, detail third — the most consistently violated rule in technical writing, and the one that wastes more engineering-hours in meetings and Slack threads than almost anything else.

chapters.title: Visual Communication and the C4 Model
chapters.summary: Diagrams that earn their place — when visuals communicate better than words, the C4 model for system architecture, UML in moderation, and the patterns that prevent diagrams from misleading.

chapters.title: Diagrams That Don't Rot
chapters.summary: Diagrams-as-code: version-controlled, PR-reviewable, and still readable when the system changes — why Mermaid, PlantUML, Structurizr, and Excalidraw beat drag-and-drop tools for long-lived architecture documentation.

chapters.title: Async Communication Patterns
chapters.summary: The default should be async — and most teams don't know how to do it well. The channel decision (Slack vs email vs doc vs meeting), writing that unblocks without a reply, and building a culture where real-time interruptions are the exception.

chapters.title: Meeting Patterns
chapters.summary: Most meetings shouldn't happen — and the ones that should are usually run wrong. The meeting types that actually serve engineering teams, how to run them efficiently, and the patterns for killing the ones that don't earn their calendar time.

chapters.title: Stakeholder and Cross-Team Communication
chapters.summary: Engineers communicate with PMs, execs, and other teams differently — same facts, different framing, different depth. The patterns for navigating the gradient from IC to leadership, and keeping cross-team work coherent.

chapters.title: Pull Request Communication
chapters.summary: The PR description is a document, a test plan, and a letter to the reviewer — all at once. Getting it right shortens review time, prevents rework, and creates a searchable record of why the code is the way it is.

chapters.title: Bad-News and Incident Communication
chapters.summary: Incidents happen. Delays happen. Scope cuts happen. The engineer who communicates them clearly and early builds trust; the one who hides or minimizes them destroys it. The patterns that make hard conversations land well.

chapters.title: Remote and AI-Augmented Communication
chapters.summary: Distributed-first communication conventions for 2026 — asynchronous by default, timezone-respectful, working out loud — and the role of LLMs as a writing accelerator without becoming a replacement for thinking.
